era_context: The early 14th century continued the fragmentation of Mongol unity, with the Yuan Dynasty firmly established in China, the Ilkhanate weakening in Persia, and the Golden Horde controlling the steppes. In Europe, feudalism was evolving into early nation-states; the Papacy remained in Avignon; and the Crusading era was in its final decline. The Islamic world saw the Mamluk Sultanate at its height, and the Indian subcontinent was dominated by the Delhi Sultanate. This was a period of significant cultural production across multiple civilizations and the beginning of European maritime expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific and Oceanic regions (minimal contemporary written record)
Central Asian nomadic peoples (limited written sources; reliant on Chinese and Islamic chronicles)

events.title: Clement V becomes Pope; Papacy moves to Avignon
events.description: Pope Clement V (r. 1305–1314) had already moved the papal seat from Rome to Avignon in 1309, establishing what would become known as the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377). By 1310, this move was consolidated, marking a significant shift in papal authority and its relationship to French royal power. The move reflected the political instability in Rome and the growing influence of the French monarchy over papal affairs. This period saw the Papacy increasingly entangled with French politics, particularly regarding the Knights Templar.
events.certainty_note: The move to Avignon is precisely dated and well-documented in papal and royal archives.

events.title: An-Nasir Muhammad becomes Mamluk Sultan
events.description: An-Nasir Muhammad al-Qalawun ascended to the Mamluk sultanate throne in 1310 (his first reign lasted until 1341, with brief interruptions). He inherited a sultanate weakened by factional conflicts among the Mamluk emirs. An-Nasir Muhammad's early reign focused on consolidating power, eliminating rival factions, and stabilizing the sultanate's military and administrative structures. His reign would become one of the longest and most significant in Mamluk history, marked by military reforms, architectural patronage, and the expansion of Mamluk commercial power.
events.certainty_note: An-Nasir Muhammad's accession date is well-documented in Mamluk historical sources.

events.title: Ghias-ud-Din Tughluq rules Delhi Sultanate
events.description: Ghias-ud-Din Tughluq (r. 1320–1325, but his rise to power began earlier) was consolidating control of the Delhi Sultanate during this period. He conducted military campaigns to expand sultanate territory and suppress rebellions. His reign marked a transition from the earlier Khalji dynasty to the Tughluq dynasty, establishing a new military and administrative order. By 1310, the sultanate was a major regional power controlling significant portions of northern India.
key_figures: Delhi Sultanate military commanders and administrators
events.certainty_note: The Delhi Sultanate's existence and general trajectory are well-documented, though specific events in 1310 are less precisely dated in surviving sources.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada's early reign
events.description: Emperor Ayurbarwada (r. 1311–1320) was not yet formally enthroned in 1310, but his rise to power was imminent. The Yuan Dynasty under his predecessor was experiencing administrative challenges and factional conflicts within the Mongol elite. By 1310, the Yuan was a well-established empire controlling China and significant portions of Asia, but internal tensions over succession and governance were mounting. Ayurbarwada's accession in 1311 would bring a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing.
key_figures: Yuan court officials and Mongol nobility

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty succession and administrative structures are well-documented in official histories, though specific events in 1310 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Ilkhanate decline and fragmentation
events.description: The Ilkhanate, the Mongol state ruling Persia and surrounding regions, was experiencing significant decline by 1310. Following the death of Öljeitü in 1316, the Ilkhanate would fragment into regional powers. By 1310, the sultanate was already weakened by factional conflicts, economic strain, and the rise of regional dynasties. This period marked the beginning of the end for Mongol unity in the Islamic world, with local Persian and Turkic powers increasingly asserting independence.

sources.name: Rashid al-Din's Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles)

events.certainty_note: The Ilkhanate's general decline is well-documented, though specific events in 1310 are less precisely dated. The fragmentation accelerated after Öljeitü's death in 1316.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of Clement V as a weak pope dominated by Philip IV is complicated by recent scholarship suggesting he exercised more agency than previously thought, particularly in his handling of the Templar affair. The Avignon Papacy is sometimes portrayed as a period of papal decline, but it also saw significant administrative reforms and cultural patronage. An-Nasir Muhammad's reign is often idealized as a 'golden age' of the Mamluks, but modern scholarship emphasizes the significant military expenditures and factional conflicts that characterized even his most successful years. The Ilkhanate's decline is sometimes attributed solely to external factors, but internal administrative failures and the loss of Mongol military cohesion were equally significant.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1310 is uneven. European events (papal succession) are richly documented in papal and royal archives. Islamic and Asian events rely on later chronicles, introducing potential dating errors. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) were compiled after the Yuan Dynasty's fall. Persian sources (Rashid al-Din) provide valuable contemporary perspective on the Ilkhanate but were written from a court perspective. African, Southeast Asian, and Oceanic regions are severely underrepresented. The bias toward literate, centralized states reflects preservation patterns rather than historical significance.

graph_edges.note: Clement V's consolidation in Avignon set the stage for the Council of Vienne and the suppression of the Knights Templar.
An-Nasir Muhammad's accession in 1310 began his long reign as Mamluk Sultan.
The Ilkhanate's decline in 1310 accelerated after Öljeitü's death in 1316, leading to fragmentation.
